author: @PyroEvil
text: The best use I see for this device is when you want to convert a mechanical source directly into heat without going through many energy conversion. Example: Windmill. Instead take mechanical energy of your windmill , convert it into electricity with a generator and from this electricty to heat with a heating element. You can just convert it from mechanical energy to heat directly with this friction heat device ( frenette heater ). I don't know if the practical ( not theorical ) effiency is better. Somebody know ?

author: @ianmiller229
text:
I accidentally boiled water in a centrifugal water pump when it got switched on while the outlet valve was closed.  The water around the impellor got so hot it melted the plastic components of the pump. Try it with your pool pump!

Any fluid will get hot if it has something agitating it violently in a confined space.
For example, auto transmissions get very hot without consuming the transmission oil.

I don’t think it is mysterious – it's friction between the impeller and the fluid. 

Unless you have a free, or very cheap, source of energy to drive the electric motor that spins the rotor inside oil (or other fluid) confined in a small space, then you might want to use that energy to produce heat by way of an electric heater.  

For example, if you had a waterwheel driving the impeller to make the fluid confined inside this unit hot, that would be cheap heat.  But using an electric motor to drive the impeller to produce heat by friction would use more electrical energy than heat produced. 

Solar power would not be much use as you likely wouldn’t need the heat on bright sunny days and storage batteries are too expensive in the capacity needed to drive a powerful enough electric motor long enough to produce useful heat.

Every time you change energy from one form to another, there is a loss of energy. So converting electrical energy into heat using friction to produce that heat, seems to be inefficient.

author: @yougeo
text:
here is a similar device that uses simple crude wood blocks as the heating surface and produces steam. it actually seems to be consuming the wood in a more efficient manner than a fire does with no flue loss obviously and therefore i wonder if its steam could power the turning wheel itself and become self sustaining until the wood was consumed. its not perpetual motion because it is consuming the wood for energy. in essence a very efficient boiler. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ROF-CImcDkc
edit2: apparently he now has it running off a steam engine from the friction heater and it requires no outside input after startup https://onedrive.live.com/?cid=A4A1D6E4BB17A7E3&id=A4A1D6E4BB17A7E3%21367&parId=A4A1D6E4BB17A7E3%21365&o=OneUp   discussion http://www.energeticforum.com/renewable-energy/2835-friction-steam-boiler-2.html


 and here is rapidly rotating magnet wheel heating up a copper pipe in seconds to extreme temperature. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8n5sSmJsCdY

edit: here is a guy runnin g one of your type devices to heat his whole home for $30 a month ! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RQjPSsIWDvg

I'm not quite sure why running a friction device from an electric motor more efficiently produces heat than running running resistance wire (or your graphene paint wall heater) . Is a resistance wire less efficient some how? What are your thoughts.

replies.author: @erdem--
replies.text: Wow, there is a lot to research in friction heating. It supposed to be more efficient when we use resistance wire, but somehow it is not. Because of ifrared radiation. when we heat a wire, it emits its own spectrum. This is why infrared heaters are more efficient.
